words: This conversation with LORA BRACKETT ALBRIGHT took place at her home on Potlatch
River below Juliaetta, Idaho, on June 23, 1976. The interviewer is SAM SCHRAGER.
SAM SCHRAGER: — know how it is that you knew that you were Republican? And
got involved in politics?
LORA ALBRIGHT: Okay. The reason that I'm a Republican is because my family: it's
a family tradition and a family philosophy, because in the old days, it
seems that the Republican Party was the party who faced up to money is
sues. Now, I inherited it, and when I was old enough to — and of course,
in those days you had to be twenty-one, you couldn't do it at eighteen,
you had to be twenty-one before you could register. So, I was married—
I was married when I was twenty and before I could vote. I got married
and I had to face up to some things. I think I voted as a family trad
ition, at first, but when my children were born and we lost our first
baby, a little boy that was tragic, as far as I was concerned. Then
our youngsters came along quickly, and in about six years I was faced
with getting my children into school. And, when you're on a homestead
or a farm and across the river and the railroad, and a few other things,
and the schools, one at Arrow and one at Pilot Rock . ... Pilot
Rock's where I taught, you see, ancl thought that I would send
him there. You can't send a six year old youngster, walking these
miles, you've got to do something about getting him to school. So, I
commenced to think about the school system. And, Sam, it was either
break up my home and go to Lewiston or get decent schools out here.
And when I say decent schools, I was looking to the future, because
the one-room schools, I didn't have anything against them if we got
good teachers. The only thing that ever limited a district school,
a one-room district school was the calibre of your citizens who were
on the school board and your teacher. And, usually it was the quality
of the teacher that you hired, and in those days, we got all of sixty
dollars a month. (Chuckles)
And there weren't many, naturally, the
other teachers, I was one that was raising my certificate, t he grade
of my certificate, and— but, anyway, - So, in order to have consol
idation of schools, you got to have roads. And in order to get roads
you had to have taxes. And when you get taxes— so I made it my busin
ess to find out all I could about school and school taxes. And, the
person that I went to— this may be interesting to you— I spent my
second year in high school in Lewiston, working for my board, and my
superintendent was Joe Jennifer, a wonderful old superintendent-teacher
Joe Jennifer. And he was elected to the legislature about that time,
because he, too, was yelling about taxes for school, for education.
And he sat down several times with me in Lewiston and he said, "Now
your open door is consolidation.' And in order to do this,"— and
then he laid it out. And so, I said, okay, "What do I get to learn
about taxes? I know who pays them, but I don't know the extent,"be-
cause that was about the time the income tax came in, y ou see. And
so I studied on that. I took to the road, and as it happened I
was appointed to the board when the State of Idaho decided that they
needed to consolidate schools, and I consolidated a 11 those schools.

speaker: SS

speaker: LA

speaker: SS

speaker: SS

speaker: LA

words: and go to Lewiston. I couldn't take my husband there, because o f his

speaker: LA

speaker: LA

speaker: SS

speaker: LA
words: in'the second grade, there's something wrong. And then I realized in

words: Uh-huh. Or right afterwards, if they were busy singing. It depended—

speaker: LA
words: if they were singing in the Nez Perce, they would be apt to get up and

words: talk any time. But, usually, they wanted the minister that was presid

words: ing to hear them. Imean, the vindication, because they expected that

speaker: LA
words: human touch, and unless the person up there heard what they said, you

speaker: LA
words: just don't respond. It's one of those things. And so two or three of

words: them might be talking at the same time and I often wondered how Star

speaker: LA
words: J. Maxwell, who was hard of hearing, and since I'm getting a little hard

speaker: LA
words: of hearing, I've often thought, "I wonder if he really heard as much

speaker: LA
words: as he thought he did?" (Chuckles)

speaker: LA
words: You know, one of those things.

speaker: LA
words: But very sincere. Very dedicated. And the young people used

speaker: LA
words: an older woman and she was the best one that they had, but the others

speaker: LA
words: would just get so bored. Of course, after you sit there for three hours

speaker: LA
words: anymore, I don't know.

speaker: SS

speaker: LA

speaker: SS

speaker: LA

words: And the Mothers' Club said, "Oh, we'll make the

speaker: LA
words: popcorn balls. Would they like that?" And, I said, "They would love it."

speaker: LA
words: "How many do we need?" I said, "Well, because that's the only Christ

speaker: LA
words: mas that many of those Indians families have, the church is always full

speaker: LA
words: and there's about five hundred people that come in there for Christmas

speaker: LA
words: Eve, so we would need three or four or five hundred." So, we got to

speaker: LA
words: thinking, "Well, gee, that's about-" They figured they could get about

speaker: LA
words: a hundred into a clothes basket, you know, - ""do we need to put strings

speaker: LA

words: as I am.! My Lord and God is willing to accept and so on!"

speaker: LA

speaker: LA

speaker: LA

speaker: LA

speaker: SS
words: Well, the revivals now, you said they were emotional. What was it like

speaker: SS
words: to those people? The revivals as an emotional experience?

speaker: LA
words: Usually a weeping spell, I mean, they were willing— this is the thing

speaker: LA
words: to me— I don't know of any white— in fact, I have been going to the

speaker: LA
words: Methodist Church up here at Kendrick since, well, it's been about twe

speaker: LA
words: lve, fifteen years now, and only once in all that time has everybody

speaker: LA
words: given what we call a testimonial meeting. White people just don't

speaker: LA
words: confess their sins. They don't recognoze them, they cover them up.

speaker: LA
words: And they would never think of standing up in church and really confes

speaker: LA
words: sing out loud what they even .considered in their own minds, that they

speaker: LA
words: consider— 'cause you know very well if you've cheated anybody you know

speaker: LA
words: whether you have lost your temper, you knowyou've sworn— and the

speaker: LA

speaker: LA
words: them, those grown men put their hands up over and cry, just actually

speaker: LA
words: cry because they felt that they had let God down. Well, now, white

speaker: LA
words: people just— if they felt it, they still wouldn't make any— I learned

speaker: LA

speaker: LA
words: It's real interesting.

speaker: SS
words: That they really talked to you.

speaker: LA

speaker: LA

speaker: LA

speaker: LA
words: and there wouldn't be any more church mergers to save you this time."

speaker: LA
words: remember, Mother, " my driving had been restricted to daylight only-

speaker: LA
words: "you can't drive after night any more. And it would be just a matter

speaker: LA
words: of going to church." And I said, "Well, I know that. But I could at

speaker: LA
words: least enjoy being with them over there maybe, and join in the worship

speaker: LA
words: service and what have you." And she said, "Well, " And I said, "As

speaker: LA
words: far as you're concerned, I'll bet you're afraid that you're going to

speaker: LA
words: be roped in on it. I won't ask you to." She's a good Presbyterian

speaker: LA

speaker: LA
words: I had hens— it depends on the - when you want to sell 'em and the first

speaker: LA
words: is always Thanksgiving Day. And so they have to be six or seven months

speaker: LA
words: old and at six and seven months I had hens that weighed twenty-five

speaker: LA

speaker: LA
words: most twenty-nine, but sometimes those larger, heavier hens was not as

speaker: LA
words: good a physical form as the little plumper ones. But the turkeys would

speaker: LA
words: be between oh, thirty-nine and forty-seven pounds at thirty-four weeks

speaker: LA
words: of age. And that grows pretty fast, you see.

speaker: LA
words: That's live weight. They would be pretty heavy. And then we were

speaker: LA
words: taking them to Spokane for a long time .and then after the kids got home

speaker: LA
words: from the war, they set up Albright's Poultry, that you've heard me

speaker: LA
words: mention. And that building up there is still Albright's Poultry, but

speaker: LA
words: it's not kept up anymore, but it's there where the old Eighteenth Street

speaker: LA
words: bridge used to come across Lewiston. And we thought that our own kids

speaker: LA
words: maybe could get the profit that Spokane was getting when we took the liveweight

speaker: LA
words: up there. We killed a lot here and dry picked for special

speaker: LA
words: orders, but a family can only do so much, and with al 1 the other feed

speaker: LA
words: ing that we had to do, we could not possibly kill the five or six thou

speaker: LA
words: sand that we would have to sell for Thanksgiving. The others went for

speaker: LA
words: Christmas and then the hens that had laid the eggs for my hatchery, then

speaker: LA
words: I always sold after the first of the year because they would have to go -

speaker: LA
words: they were not first grade birds, you see, I mean, they were no longer

speaker: LA
words: those first thirty-eight weeks, because they'd been a year in the laying

speaker: LA
words: pens. And yet, here they were just one year old and the hens were nice

speaker: LA
words: big hens and good hens. So then, we would be killing turkeys probably

speaker: LA
words: until February, March and what have you, because we wanted to get rid

speaker: LA
words: of them, because then by that time I was starting the new cy cle. I

speaker: LA
words: would have — you can't sell your hens until you get the eggs that you

speaker: LA
words: want, you see, and get them hatched. And so, it was really quite some

speaker: LA
words: thing. And after they get through the laying season some of them would

speaker: LA
words: be scarred and some of them would be hurt and what have you. And they

speaker: LA
words: wouldn't be as fat as young ones, you know. So you have to take

speaker: LA
words: second grade price, and sometimes they would grade up and we got so

speaker: LA
words: we could tell. But after the boys came home from the war they estab

speaker: LA

speaker: LA
words: to do any killing out here, you see, we just them all down

speaker: LA
words: anyway, and they didn't want it, and so, we just, as I say— when I was

speaker: LA
words: away they sold my hens and I said, "Okay, kids, that's it." That's how

speaker: SS
words: ure around in the country.

speaker: LA

speaker: LA
words: or not, because it was a T'Thite conference, I mean a Methodist Confer

speaker: LA

speaker: LA

speaker: LA

speaker: LA
words: Mrs. Albright can you add anything to that?" And I said, "No, Stephen

speaker: LA
words: has done very well. This is it. We are there." And, I said, "The only

speaker: LA
words: thing I could mention has been mentioned that the women meet once a month

speaker: LA
words: for a study and a worship. Of course, we meet every week to work up the

speaker: LA
words: things in the house, to keep it clean and we have these public dinners."

speaker: LA
words: Oh, land, everybody comes in, you know and eats and if they feel like it

speaker: LA

speaker: LA

speaker: LA
words: but, anyway I said, "Well, this is fine. We'll be here and we'd like

speaker: LA
words: to have you come .back again and we're glad that you came." And all of

speaker: LA
words: this. And so they turned it back to Stephen Reuben and this is what

speaker: LA
words: he said— I mean, the White person had made an aside, let's say, saying,

speaker: LA
words: "Well, when you get to Heaven you'll have a star in your crown.!: -'cr

speaker: LA
words: the work that I was doing over there with the Indians, you know. And I

speaker: LA
words: don't know whether Stephen heard her or just what, but he got up and he

speaker: LA
words: made this remark, he said, "We thank God for .: Mrs. Albright. You

speaker: LA
words: know she is young woman"- his English, he'd leave off his—"she is young

speaker: LA
words: woman, but someday she die. But probably I die first, and I will b e up

speaker: LA
words: there in Heaven, and when she comes to the pearly gate, I will say,to

speaker: LA
words: St. Peter, "You let her in, she's a good woman!""

speaker: LA
words: And sat down. Well,

speaker: LA
words: there I sat with my thumb .in my mouth practically, you know through

speaker: LA
words: all this, and those White people just roared, and I was so embarrassed,

speaker: LA
words: because of the idea. And so, everybody looked at me, you know, and T'

speaker: LA
words: LORA ALBRIGHT: Sophie Corbett was one of the older women, and she was the one

speaker: LA
words: that could spank the children and get away with it. Indians don't spank

speaker: LA
words: their children, as a rule. They're special. They're very special. Their

speaker: LA
words: children just mean everything to them, or they did in those days, I don't

speaker: LA
words: know how it is now. And, when they were noisy , especially in the church,

speaker: LA
words: why she would scold them. And then, if they didn't be good, why she

speaker: LA
words: would go up there and grab hold of them and slap them! She wasn't above

speaker: LA
words: slapping them. She was going to have order in that church or know why.

speaker: LA
words: And I had to laugh two or three times, because I overheard some eleven

speaker: LA
words: year olds; ten, eleven year olds, talking outside on the— this is dur

speaker: LA
words: ing summer school, you know when you have Bible School and that. We

speaker: LA
words: were having this vacation Bible School over there, and one of them said,

speaker: LA
words: "You better be careful,'cause Sophia's here today and she'll slap you if

speaker: LA
words: you don't behave!" (Chuckles) I never let on I heard at all. I don't

speaker: LA
words: know what going to happen over there. I wanted to go back, but my own

speaker: LA
words: kids didn't want me to and so I didn't.
